explanation: The input states that if the external ADC fails to initialize or reports an error condition after a fixed number of retries (specifically after 5 tries), the controller must go to fail-safe mode. We have these atomic propositions: ADCConnFailure (ADC fails to initialize), ADCError (ADC reports an error), ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5 (ADC fails to initialize or reports error 5 or more times), and failSafeMode (controller is in fail-safe mode). We are not allowed to use numerical comparisons in LTL, so we can't directly say 'after 5 retries'. Instead, we must use the available atomic proposition 'ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5'. Thus, the property must say: Whenever the system enters StartUpMode, if ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5 becomes true (i.e., the ADC fails or errors after enough retries), then eventually the controller must enter failSafeMode. Formally: Globally, StartUpMode and ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5 implies that eventually failSafeMode holds. This most directly captures the intent, given the available atomic propositions and since we cannot encode the counting in LTL directly without such an atomic proposition. Previous LTL properties (if any) not using this special atomic proposition would not precisely capture the 'after 5 retries' logic.

The requirement specifies that if, in Start-up Mode, the external ADC either fails to initialize or reports an error, and this persists for the maximum number of retries (five), the controller must transition to fail-safe mode. The closest available atomic propositions are StartUpMode (system in start-up), ADCConnFailure (ADC fails to initialize), ADCError (ADC error), and ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5 (ADC failed at least 5 times or errors at least 5 times). So, if during startup you get a sequence where, after at least 5 such failures, the controller must eventually enter failSafeMode. This is best captured using: globally, if in StartUpMode and ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5, then eventually failSafeMode. This captures both the persistence and that the error triggers the transition at any point during start-up mode. This formulation uses only provided atomic propositions, not direct numerical comparisons, and expresses the requirement as an LTL implication with an eventuality for triggering fail-safe mode.

Note: Previous encodings might miss the necessity of the implication being available at any point during startup mode, and the need to eventually transition to fail-safe mode as a consequence of the error condition with enough failed attempts.

The input requires that after a certain number of ADC initialization or error attempts, the system transitions to fail-safe mode from start-up mode. The most relevant atomic propositions are: StartUpMode (system is in start-up), ADCConnFailure (ADC fails to initialize), ADCError (ADC reports error), ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5 (ADC fails to initialize or reports an error condition for 5 or more retries), and failSafeMode (controller in fail-safe mode). The specification can be captured by stating: Globally, if the system is in Start-up Mode and there are 5 or more retries due to ADC connection failure or error, then eventually failSafeMode should be entered. This can be written in LTL as: G((StartUpMode & ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5) -> F(failSafeMode)). Previous formulations that omit the explicit check for StartUpMode, or that use only local steps (e.g., X) would not accurately capture the 'after a fixed number of retries' condition during system start-up, nor would they address the requirement for eventual transition to failSafeMode, as needed by the spec.

The natural language states that if, during Start-up Mode, the external ADC either fails to initialize (ADCConnFailure) or reports an error (ADCError) for a maximum of 5 retries (which can be indicated by ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5), then the controller must transition to fail-safe mode (failSafeMode). As LTL does not permit the use of numerical comparison operators, but an atomic proposition 'ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5' is provided to represent this bounded retry condition. 

Thus, the property is: Whenever the system is in StartUpMode, and ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5 is true, then eventually the system must reach failSafeMode. This is expressed as G((StartUpMode & ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5) -> F(failSafeMode)). This LTL formula captures the required semantics without comparing numbers (since 'ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5' is itself an atomic proposition). Other approaches such as directly expressing a count or using release/until with counters would not be possible with LTL and the given restrictions, so this is the most direct mapping.

The requirement specifies that if, during Start-up Mode, the external ADC either fails to initialize or reports an error condition after a maximum of 5 retries, then the controller must transition to the fail-safe mode. The available atomic propositions are: StartUpMode (start-up mode is active), ADCConnFailure (ADC fails to initialize), ADCError (ADC reports an error), ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5 (ADC error or failure for 5 or more retries), and failSafeMode (fail-safe mode active).

The most direct encoding with these is: Whenever the system is in Start-up Mode and the ADC error/failure condition (after 5 or more retries) occurs (ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5), then eventually the controller transitions to failSafeMode. This is written with the LTL formula: G((StartUpMode & ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5) -> F(failSafeMode)).

This correctly captures the requirement by ensuring that whenever (globally) during start-up mode the failure condition (including retries) occurs, then eventually (at some point after) the fail-safe mode will be entered. Alternative formulas would be incorrect if they do not combine the Start-up Mode, the ADC error/failure with retries, or fail to ensure the transition to fail-safe mode following this specific scenario.

The input requirement specifies: In Start-up Mode, if the external ADC fails to initialize or reports an error condition after a fixed number of retries (maximum 5), the controller shall transition to the fail-safe mode. Given the atomic propositions, 'StartUpMode' can be used to indicate the start-up phase, 'ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5' indicates that the ADC has either failed to initialize or reported an error condition for 5 or more retries, and 'failSafeMode' indicates the transition to the fail-safe mode. To capture the intended property: 'Whenever the system is in StartUpMode and the ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5 becomes true, eventually failSafeMode must be entered.' In LTL, this is expressed as: G((StartUpMode & ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5) -> F failSafeMode). This ensures that if the failure condition is met during start-up, the controller will eventually enter fail-safe mode. Previous properties that do not combine both the StartUpMode and the retry threshold condition as triggers for failSafeMode will not accurately capture the timing and context dependencies stated in the requirement.

The requirement states: 'If the external ADC fails to initialize or reports an error condition after a fixed number of retries (maximum 5), the controller shall transition to the fail-safe mode.' 

From the atomic propositions, we see that 'ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5' indicates the external ADC fails or errors for 5 or more retries, and 'failSafeMode' indicates the fail-safe mode is active.

So, we need to state that: Whenever the ADC has reached 5 or more retries (i.e., ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5 happens), the system must (eventually) enter fail safe mode (failSafeMode). Expressed in LTL: 'G (ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5 -> F failSafeMode)'. 

This universally applies throughout the system: at any time the ADC has failed 5+ retries, failSafeMode must eventually happen, which captures the requirement. This property does NOT encode that the system must *immediately* transition to fail-safe mode, but only that it will eventually do so. It also captures both failure modes described (fail to initialize or reports error for 5 or more retries), as both are considered by ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5.

Alternative translations that refer to 'initFail' or try to separate error vs fail-to-initiate are unnecessary here: the single atomic proposition 'ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5' captures the required precondition. There are no better atomic propositions that more faithfully capture this behavior.

Therefore, the LTL property most likely to capture the sentence is: 'G (ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5 -> F failSafeMode)'.

The input natural language requires that if, during Start-up Mode, the external ADC either fails to initialize or reports an error condition for 5 or more retries (i.e., the condition ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5 holds), then the controller must transition to fail-safe mode (failSafeMode). In LTL, a typical way to express this causality is by using the 'Globally' (G) operator to say that whenever (StartUpMode & ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5) occurs, it is always followed by (or at the same time as, or in the next state) failSafeMode being active 'eventually' (F). Thus, the property G((StartUpMode & ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5) -> F failSafeMode) captures that from any point in time onwards, if StartUpMode and ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5 are true, then at some future point, failSafeMode must also become true. This is the best encoding with the available atomic propositions and LTL operators, since propositions about the number of retries are already encoded in ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5, and no explicit transition is required, just the guarantee of eventual failSafeMode entry after the error condition is met. No previous LTL properties were provided, so no comparison is needed.

The natural language requirement states: If the external ADC fails to initialize or reports an error condition after a fixed number of retries (maximum 5), the controller shall transition to the fail-safe mode (from Start-up Mode). The relevant atomic propositions are ADCConnFailure (external ADC fails to initialize), ADCError (external ADC error), ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5 (ADC failure or error for 5 or more retries), StartUpMode (system is in Start-up Mode), and failSafeMode (system is in fail-safe mode). 

In LTL, we encode this as: Whenever the system is in StartUpMode and the ADC has failed or reported error for 5 or more retries (ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5 becomes true), then eventually the failSafeMode must become true (the controller must enter fail-safe mode at some point in the future). This is formalized by "Globally: if StartUpMode & ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5 then eventually failSafeMode" (G((StartUpMode & ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5) -> F failSafeMode)).

This captures the requirement correctly, as it only triggers a required response (failSafeMode) when in Start-up and the error/condition of ADC exceeds 5 retries. Previous patterns such as triggering simply on ADCConnFailure or ADCError do not cover the retry semantics, nor do they restrict the behavior to when in Start-up mode. This property leverages the summaries implied by the available APs, especially ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5, to capture the conditional logic required by the NL statement.

The natural language states that: If (1) the external ADC fails to initialize OR (2) the external ADC reports an error condition, for a maximum of 5 retries, then the controller shall transition to the fail-safe mode. There is a single atomic proposition ('ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5') that signals when the ADC fails or errors at least five times. Thus, we want the system to ensure: whenever in Start-up Mode, if this error/retry threshold is reached, the system shall (eventually) enter fail-safe mode. This is captured by the LTL formula: Globally, if we are in StartUpMode and ADCRetries_greaterThanOrEqual_5 holds, then eventually failSafeMode holds. This ensures that as soon as the 5th retry is reached (in Start-up), the transition to fail-safe mode happens.

No numeric comparisons are used; only the provided atomic propositions and standard LTL operators are included.
